input_semantic_node: Abortion moral justifiability via fetal personhood extension.
central_semantic_node: Moral Equivalence of Fetal and Adult Rights in Abortion Jurisprudence.
argument_reasons: The input presents a nested circular context where personhood is defined by conception, which then grants rights equal to an adult, used as a premise to evaluate the moral justifiability of abortion.
The presence of a self-referential loop (fetus as person from conception used to define a fetus as a person from conception) serves as an axiomatic anchor rather than a derived conclusion.

ontological_signature.function: Defining the legal and ethical boundaries of bodily autonomy vs. right to life.
ontological_signature.tension: Conflict between the 'Right to Life' (fetal) and 'Bodily Integrity' (maternal), both formulated as absolute rights.
ontological_signature.limit: Dependent on the axiomatic definition of 'person' which lacks empirical consensus.

conceptual_disintegration.definition: A growing life inside a mother that is not yet born.
A being that has the highest level of protection and special values.
Rules that say what you can or cannot do to someone because they are important.
Having a very good reason that makes an action okay.

preconceptual_transduction: If we say a tiny seed is the same as a big tree right now, then we have to treat the seed just like the tree. But if the seed needs to stay in someone else's garden to grow, we have to decide if the seed's life is more important than the gardener's choice about their own land.

iterations.base_statement: A fetus has the same moral rights as an adult
iterations.context: Undefined because 'rights' are socially/legally constructed and vary by jurisdiction.

global_evaluation.criteria: The argument uses its conclusion (personhood from conception) to define its premise, creating a closed logical loop.

deductive_observations.origin: P: Fetus is a person from conception.
deductive_observations.conclusion: If P is true, then harming the fetus is equivalent to harming an adult.
deductive_observations.notes: Requires 'personhood' to be the sole determinant of moral status.

subjectivities: The definition of 'conception' as the start of 'personhood' is a metaphysical/religious claim not universally shared.

counterexamples.refuted_statement: A fetus has the same moral rights as an adult
counterexamples.description: Judith Jarvis Thomson’s violinist analogy: Even if one has a right to life, they do not have a right to use another person's body against their will.
counterexamples.notes: Separates 'right to life' from 'right to use another's body'.

inductive_observations.observed_pattern: Legal systems often treat fetal death differently than adult homicide (penalty variation).
inductive_observations.inference: Societies do not truly believe in absolute ontological equivalence.

preconceptual_conclusion: The rules depend on when you decide a person starts being a person, but you can't use the rule to prove the rule.
emerging_theory_or_intuition: The 'Violinist Overlap': Rights do not exist in a vacuum; the intersection of two 'absolute' rights (Life vs. Autonomy) creates a computational irreducibility that cannot be resolved without an external axiomatic tie-breaker.

rows: A fetus is a person from conception
Self-referential loop within the provided context; it is an axiom, not a provable statement.
A fetus has the same moral rights as an adult
Ambiguous; depends on the definition of 'person' which is currently undefined.
Contingent on the outcome of the conflict between fetal personhood and maternal autonomy.

formula_dictionary.A: A fetus is a person from conception
formula_dictionary.B: A fetus has the same moral rights as an adult

why_the_formula_structure_is_incorrect: The formula assumes that B (equal rights) automatically excludes C (justifiability), ignoring the 'Right to Bodily Autonomy' which may override B.
boolean_formula_to_natural_language: If a fetus is a person (A) and has adult rights (B), and there is no superior right to bodily autonomy (D), then abortion is not morally justifiable (!C).
conclusion: If fetal personhood is granted at conception then moral equivalence requires treating the fetus as an end in itself, yet this conflicts with the principle of bodily integrity as seen in cases like McFall v. Shimp (1978) where no person is legally forced to donate body parts to save another, and for all this the conclusion is that the moral justifiability of abortion depends entirely on whether one prioritizes the right to life as a positive claim on others' bodies or as a negative right against interference. In a single line.

collapse_implications.statement: A fetus has the same moral rights as an adult
collapse_implications.implication_by_false_state: Abortion becomes a matter of medical hygiene or minor ethical concern.
collapse_implications.implication_by_true_state: Abortion is framed as a conflict between two equal human beings.

logical_tension.paradox: The Unbound Participant: To protect the 'person', one must violate the 'person' holding it.
logical_tension.ambiguity: Does the 'Right to Life' include the right to occupy another's internal organs?
logical_tension.useful_contradiction: A fetus is both 'part' of the mother (biologically) and 'separate' from the mother (genetically), making it a dual-state entity.

analogous_reorganization: Compulsory organ donation to a genetic match.
The Sovereign and the Guest: Hospitality vs. Ownership.
Quantum entanglement where measurement (legal ruling) forces one state to collapse while disrupting the other.
implications: Granting 'personhood' to early-stage biological processes necessitates a total re-evaluation of all medical practices involving embryos (IVF, research).
If bodily autonomy is not absolute, the state acquires a precedent to mandate other biological interventions.

global_reevaluation.criteria: The prompt structures the justification within a closed loop of its own premises; the argument can only be 'true' if the circular axiom is accepted without external validation.

reconclusion: The proposition 'Abortion is morally justifiable' is rendered 'False' within the strict, closed-loop logic of the provided context because the context enforces an axiomatic equivalence between a fetus and an adult; however, this logic is epistemologically fragile as it relies on a self-referential definition of personhood that ignores the competing axiom of bodily autonomy.
preconceptual_reconclusion: The circle of words says it is wrong because it starts by saying a seed is a child. If you agree with the start, you agree with the end, but the start is just a choice of how we name things.
causal_chain: Determining moral justifiability requires defining rights holders which leads to the axiom of personhood at conception which leads to the claim of rights equivalence between fetuses and adults which creates a zero-sum conflict with maternal bodily autonomy which cannot be resolved within the provided circular logic because the conclusion is baked into the initial definition of the fetus as an adult-equivalent person and therefore the logic is a closed loop that excludes external human rights frameworks and for all this the argument is a demonstration of formal internal consistency achieved through external empirical exclusion.
context: Abortion is morally justifiable (context: A fetus is a person from conception (context: A fetus has the same moral rights as an adult (context: A fetus is a person from conception (context: Is abortion morally justifiable?))))
